retrieved_docs.title: Triple Arthrodesis -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Equipment for the triple arthrodesis includes standard hardware for most arthrodesis procedures of the rearfoot and ankle. Screws from 6.5 mm to 8mm screws may serve for fixation of the subtalar joint, 4.5 mm screws may be used to fixate the midtarsal joints.  Specialty equipment from many manufacturers exists, include plates and staples for the midtarsal joint. A thigh tourniquet is used for hemostasis during the procedure, and intraoperative fluoroscopy is recommended for confirmation of joint positioning and proper fixation. A distraction device such as a lamina spreader or Hintermann distractor may be useful.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Arthroplasty -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Second-generation implants were developed to combat the large bone cuts required in first-generation, which were fixed bearing with a polyethylene bearing surface built into the talar or tibial components. Examples of second-generation implants included the Buechel-Pappas Total Ankle Replacement (Endotec, South Orange, NJ) in the United States and the Scandinavian Total Ankle Replacement (STAR; Waldemar Link, Hamburg, Germany) in Europe. This generation had more conservative bone cuts to allow for more bone preservation and were press fit. Like the first-generation models, however, there was a high failure rate due to increased polyethylene wear.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical Oxygen -- Equipment -- Classification of Topically Applied Oxygen Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Both systems are similar in that they use robust compression-plate oxygen generators (immune to backpressure from improper offloading), are similar in size and weight, and have oxygen distribution systems for even distribution of oxygen across the wound bed. One of the systems uses a fixed flow of oxygen delivered through an oxygen distribution system (ODS) that consists of tubing connected to a diffusive membrane in the shape of a wheel.  The ODS is placed above the wound bed and underneath a moist wound therapy dressing chosen by the wound care provider.  The other system uses an integrated oxygen distribution system within an oxygen diffusion dressing (ODD) available in multiple sizes, with or without an adhesive border. This system does not require using other dressings yet can be used with any non-occlusive dressing. The ODD system also has the ability to vary the oxygen flow rate, which can aid in the management of exudate and pain levels and monitor the pressure in the wound bed.  The ODD system monitors wound bed pressure and do not allow the pressure in the wound bed to exceed 20 mm Hg, which is at the lowest reported levels of capillary collapse in skin tissue. [10] This may be of significant benefit since it ensures that the localized pressure does not limit the underlying flow of nutrients and blood to the tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthopedic Implant Materials -- Issues of Concern -- Common Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Stainless steel has been employed in the design of countless historical and modern implants for orthopedic care. It is frequently employed in the manufacturing of orthopedics plates, screws, sliding hip screws, some flexible nails, and early generation rigid intramedullary nails. Cerclage cables are also routinely made of braided stainless steel wires. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Femur Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Open Reduction Internal Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: The 95-degree blade plate is a useful implant for fracture fixation of OTA 33 type A patterns, as well as for the stabilization of malunions and nonunions.  The placement of the blade plate is often a technically demanding procedure because three planes must simultaneously be corrected. Condylar malalignment may result if the chisel and plate are incorrectly inserted. Sliding barrel condylar plates are devices based on the compression screw used for hip fractures. These devices allow for the ability to apply compression across the femoral condyles. They allow better purchase in osteoporotic bone and only need two planes of correction when compared to the blade plate. Potential disadvantages with this construct include its bulky size and the requirement to remove a large amount of bone from the lateral femoral condyle. Due to its bulky size, the plate may cause symptomatic hardware and knee symptoms. Clinical studies have generally shown good results with fixed angled blade plate and sliding barrel condylar plate devices with 82% reporting satisfactory to excellent results at 9.5 years for condylar plate and 81% good or excellent results for the dynamic condylar screw. [20] [21] A recent study comparing the less invasive stabilization system (LISS) with minimally invasive sliding barrel condylar plates in the treatment of OTA 33 type A1 to C2 concluded that there was no advantage for locking plate designs. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pelvic Binders -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The method of application may explain why pelvic binders are frequently malpositioned. Studies suggest that placing the device under the lumbar spine before sliding caudally results in too cephalad a position, often resting over the iliac crests. Instead, the binder should be placed under the knees without significant movement of the legs. Clothing over the hip and thigh regions should be removed if possible, as these can contribute to accelerated pressure damage. The knees should be brought together, and the limbs internally rotated before the binder is placed at the level of the greater trochanters and tightened according to the manufacturer's instruction. [22] The patient's knees and ankles may also be bound together to assist internal rotation at the hips. [24] Where a coexisting femoral fracture results in the shortening of the limb, a figure-of-eight bandage can be tied around the foot and ankle to splint the pelvis and the femur.

retrieved_docs.title: Acetabular Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Reduction and Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Except for both column fractures, standard fracture reduction sequence is first to reduce and stabilize displaced column fractures and then reduce any wall fracture After definitive fixation of reduced fragments, entire construct stabilized with buttress plates For both column fractures, first reduce and stabilize one of the columns to the axial skeleton (iliac wing), then the other column, and later, if present, the wall component. The entire construct then gets stabilized with buttress plates Columns may be stabilized in young, healthy bone using screws alone; osteopenic bone and all wall fractures require buttress plating

retrieved_docs.title: Forearm Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Dynamic Compression Plate
retrieved_docs.content: Dynamic compression plating (DCP) has been considered the gold standard treatment in forearm fractures. [21] DCP provides axial compression using an eccentrically placed screw; this screw has a congruent head to plate hole fit. The implant has been used in different modes like compression, neutralization, buttress plate, and tension band. It provides anatomical reduction and absolute stability by compression. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Femur Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Open Reduction Internal Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: A condylar buttress plate utilizes multiple lags screws for the treatment of condylar fractures.  Advantages to these plates allow for the treatment of OTA 33 type B fractures.  Disadvantages to these plates include not being a fixed-angle device and thereby relying on the friction of the bone-plate interface.  Due to the eccentric mechanical axis of the femur, screw loosening can occur, causing the typical varus deformity. Locking Plates

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pelvic Binders -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: While a hemodynamically unstable patient presenting with a pelvic fracture is not suitable for angiography, the patient will often be a candidate for pelvic packing. The binder is often exchanged for an anterior-based external fixator to pack against. [31] In this situation, there is concern that an anterior external fixator will inadequately control the posterior pelvic ring unless combined with a C-clamp or posterior pelvic fixation. Recent literature suggests that the pelvic binder can be maintained, and pelvic packing performed with the binder continuing to apply circumferential compression, with no differences in transfusion requirement or mortality compared to an external fixator. [32] In another study, a pelvic binder outperformed the use of a C-clamp, resulting in faster hemostasis with reduced mortality. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthopedic Implant Materials -- Issues of Concern -- Common Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Titanium is a common material in a variety of orthopedic implants. Most total hip femoral stem components are made of a titanium alloy. The same can be said of most total shoulder arthroplasty stems. Furthermore, nearly all currently used intramedullary rods are composed of titanium alloy. Traditional stainless steel plating systems have been in use for decades for fracture stabilization.  Many surgeons can choose either stainless steel or titanium plates and screws. There has been much research regarding decreasing bacterial adherence to titanium, which may be more prone to allowing adherence. They are also employed in a variety of spine surgery applications, including some pedicle screws, rods, and interbody devices. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Splinting -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Before splint application, any fracture-dislocation should be reduced. Stockinette is cut to cover the leg so that there is extra past the toes and past the knee.  The extra stockinette will be folded down over the plaster which allows the creation of padded cuffs at the ends of the splint for patient comfort. Cast padding is used to wrap directly over the stockinette, beginning from the metatarsal heads and continuing all of the way up to the tibial tubercle. Care must be taken to sufficiently pad the medial and lateral malleoli and heel to prevent pressure ulcers from developing in the splint. The plaster splinting material for the posterior mold is wet, wrung, bonded, and placed on the plantar aspect of the foot, extending up to below the popliteal fossa. Cast padding may be used to wrap over the plaster to hold it in place while the remainder of the plaster is applied. The plaster splinting material for the stirrup is wet, wrung, bonded, and placed starting on the medial aspect of the leg, wrapping around the plantar aspect of the heel and up the lateral aspect of the leg. Cast padding may be used to wrap over the plaster splinting material once more, and the excess stockinette at the ends of the splint may be folded down to produce a cuff. An elastic bandage is used to wrap over the splint. Once wrapped with the elastic bandage, molding may begin. Molding is necessary for ankle dislocations or subluxations to produce a buttress to keep the ankle joint reduced. It is also important to ensure that the ankle joint is in neutral dorsiflexion. Splinting the ankle in a plantarflexed position will result in an equinus contracture of the heel cord.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertebral Augmentation -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: A balloon tamp or the standard "kit" This is from the manufacturer for whatever system is being used.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Bone Immobilization -- Issues of Concern -- Types of Splints
retrieved_docs.content: Pelvic circumferential compression devices (pelvic binder) work to provide circumferential stabilization of the entire pelvic cavity. This is performed to reduce pelvic fractures and decrease pelvic volume, in turn leading to decreased hemorrhage. The compression force is applied at the level of the femoral trochanters. This can also be achieved by wrapping a sheet around the pelvis and tying it tightly to secure it in place.

retrieved_docs.title: Reverse Shoulder Arthroplasty -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: A cementless glenoid base plate; is seated on the prepared glenoid and fixed by multidirectional screws. The modular geosphere is usually half a sphere and is secured at the glenoid base plate. The humeral stem is inserted into the prepared humerus. Two types of stems are available: cemented and cementless press-fit stems. The humeral cup is fitted on top of the humeral stem. It is designed to secure the concave insert on top of it. A concave polyethylene insert is fitted on top of the humeral cup. It is concave and articulates with the geosphere.

retrieved_docs.title: Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis -- Treatment / Management -- In situ fixation with Percutaneous Screws
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic fixation of the contralateral hip is another controversial modality. It is indicated when there are high-risk factors for a contralateral slip. Such as obesity, male gender, initial slip in a younger than ten years old, endocrine disorders

Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical planning, and coordinated management of fractures and bone‑density disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is highlighted as a versatile orthopedic technique that stabilizes bones without prior reduction and is applied not only to the femur and hip but also to other lower‑extremity bones, the radius and ulna, and the clavicle. Collectively, these entities illustrate the extensive array of surgical options—ranging from fixation, reduction, and growth arrest to osteoplastic remodeling, prosthetic revision, soft‑tissue repair, and prophylaxis—available for managing structural, functional, and developmental problems of the thigh and leg.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category aggregates a wide array of operative interventions that target the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated joint structures. Direct femoral procedures include internal fixation of the femur (with or without fracture reduction), intramedullary nailing, open reduction of femur fractures (with or without internal fixation), suture of the periosteum of the femur, repair of distal femur malunion, femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and femoral shortening performed with a blade plate. Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus. Reconstructive

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: The procedural category **THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS** encompasses a comprehensive set of operative interventions that address fractures, dislocations, deformities, limb‑length discrepancies, prosthetic failures, and reconstructive needs of the hip, femur, tibia, fibula, and adjacent lower‑extremity structures. Direct‑site femoral procedures include **internal fixation of femur** (with or without fracture reduction), **intramedullary nailing of femur**, **open reduction of fracture of femur** (with or without internal fixation), **femoral shortening with blade plate**, **repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck**, and various **osteoplasties of femur** (plain, lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with femoral segment transfer). **Trochanterplasty** and **femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are also performed directly on the femur. Growth‑plate modulation techniques such as **arrest, epiphyseal, any method

retrieved_docs.content: fractures, and radius/ulna fractures; it is grouped with femoral‑head reconstruction in the clinical subgroup **هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)**, which addresses osteoporosis and low bone density, and also includes “other fractures.” Collectively, these relationships illustrate an integrated surgical framework that addresses fracture management, deformity correction, growth‑plate modulation, prosthetic revision, and bone‑weakening disorders, all centered on precise anatomical targeting of the femur, distal femur, proximal tibia, and fibula to achieve optimal structural correction and functional recovery.

Collectively, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of femoral surgical care—from acute fracture fixation and malunion repair to limb‑lengthening, shortening, growth‑plate modulation, prosthetic revision, and prophylactic measures—providing clinicians with a hierarchical framework for selecting the appropriate procedure based on anatomical site, desired mechanical outcome, and overall treatment context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions for the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated lower‑extremity structures. Child procedures include growth‑modulating surgeries such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which alter physeal activity to correct angular deformities. Fracture‑stabilization techniques directly targeting the femur are represented by “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation.” Osteoplastic procedures modify femoral length or contour and include “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
retrieved_docs.content: technique across skeletal regions and is grouped with hip‑head replacement and multiple fractures within the osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام) clinical category, reflecting the heightened need for surgical stabilization in patients with reduced bone density. Collectively, this network of femoral‑focused and lower‑extremity surgical options provides clinicians with comprehensive strategies for fracture stabilization, malunion correction, growth disturbance management, limb‑length modification, prosthetic revision, joint reconstruction, and soft‑tissue repair, thereby supporting optimal functional outcomes across a spectrum of orthopedic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 154
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category encompasses a wide array of operative interventions that address deformities, fractures, malunions, prosthetic complications, and growth‑plate abnormalities of the femur, tibia, fibula, and proximal hip structures. Within this framework, epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—target the growth plates of the distal femur, proximal tibia, and fibula to halt or partially halt growth and correct angular deformities such as genu varus or valgus. Fracture management of the femur includes “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open

Collectively, the knowledge base connects clavicular surgery to a comprehensive shoulder‑and‑arm operative framework, aligns closed‑fracture management with underlying osteoporosis, and integrates internal fixation and hip replacement techniques across multiple skeletal regions, thereby supporting coordinated orthopedic decision‑making and patient‑centered care.

This integrated structure aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical strategies, recognizing the influence of bone health on fracture risk, and documenting procedures within standardized orthopedic classification systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.
